sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: Attribution training during intergroup collaboration. Researchers examined whether brief attribution training changes interpretations of ambiguous behavior during an intergroup teamwork task using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 240 students randomly assigned to complete mixed-group problem solving after different preparatory modules and compared Control, Attribution training, Contact + training. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in exposure to dispositional versus situational explanations and structured cooperative contact, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was hostile attribution score after reading ambiguous teammate feedback; a secondary measure was willingness to work with the same teammate again. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring prior intergroup contact, baseline prejudice ratings, and social desirability scores, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that hostile attribution scores were highest in the control group and lowest when attribution training was combined with structured contact. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, social desirability or preexisting openness to intergroup contact could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact reported greater change in hostile attribution ratings and willingness for future collaboration than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether baseline intergroup anxiety altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be adding behavioral follow-up choices and blinded coding of team interactions rather than relying only on vignette ratings. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome.
questions.stem: Which interpretation of Figure 1 is most consistent with the investigators' central claim?

choices.A: The figure proves that social desirability or preexisting openness to intergroup contact played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.
choices.B: The study cannot support any inference about attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact because psychological and social constructs cannot be operationalized with measurable outcomes.
choices.C: The safest conclusion is that the comparison group with the lowest mean performed best, because smaller bars or lower scores are always favorable in behavioral figures. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.D: The outcome pattern supports the claim that changing attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact can alter hostile attribution score after reading ambiguous teammate feedback in this setting, while still leaving some alternative mechanisms unresolved.

explanations.A: This choice is tempting because it highlights a real issue in research on attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact, but it does not fit the passage as well as the credited answer. The option says, 'The figure proves that social desirability or preexisting openness to intergroup contact played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.' Yet the study manipulated exposure to dispositional versus situational explanations and structured cooperative contact and measured hostile attribution score after reading ambiguous teammate feedback, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. The passage leaves room for social desirability or preexisting openness to intergroup contact, but that limitation does not reverse the observed pattern or erase the construct being tested.

explanations.D: Correct. This answer best integrates the design, Figure 1, and the construct being tested. The passage says the groups differed in exposure to dispositional versus situational explanations and structured cooperative contact and that the primary outcome was hostile attribution score after reading ambiguous teammate feedback; the figure then shows that hostile attribution scores were highest in the control group and lowest when attribution training was combined with structured contact. That combination supports the inference in the answer without overstating it as proof of every possible mechanism. It also respects the distinction between a construct, such as attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact, and the way that construct was operationalized through hostile attribution ratings and willingness for future collaboration and the measured outcome. On MCAT-style P/S questions, the best answer usually matches the specific comparison and measurement strategy rather than choosing a familiar keyword in isolation.

questions.stem: In this study, hostile attribution ratings and willingness for future collaboration is best described as which type of variable?

choices.A: It is the independent variable only because all self-report measures are automatically assigned by the researcher rather than measured from participants.
choices.B: It is a biological confound that makes the social construct irrelevant, since subjective ratings can never be used in behavioral research.
choices.C: It is an operationalized measure of attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact, meaning it is a concrete indicator used to study a broader theoretical construct.
choices.D: It is the sampling frame, because any questionnaire completed before the outcome determines which participants entered the study. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

questions.stem: Which additional finding would most strengthen the proposed mechanism rather than merely repeating the same outcome?

choices.A: Showing that every participant preferred the intervention would prove the mechanism, even if preference was unrelated to hostile attribution score after reading ambiguous teammate feedback.
choices.B: Showing that baseline scores differed strongly between groups would strengthen the causal interpretation by making the groups easier to separate.
choices.C: Removing the manipulation check would strengthen the study because fewer measures always reduce the risk of measurement bias. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.D: Showing that changes in hostile attribution ratings and willingness for future collaboration statistically mediate the effect of condition on hostile attribution score after reading ambiguous teammate feedback would better connect the manipulation to the proposed mechanism.

questions.stem: Which limitation should most temper the authors' conclusion about attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact?

choices.A: The design does not fully rule out social desirability or preexisting openness to intergroup contact, so the result supports the authors' interpretation but does not uniquely identify one mechanism.
choices.B: The study is invalid because any use of a comparison condition prevents researchers from drawing conclusions about group differences. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.C: The finding is impossible to interpret because a significant behavioral outcome can never coexist with an imperfect manipulation check.
choices.D: The result must be due to random error because all social and psychological interventions produce identical effects across participants.

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.B: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats attribution bias, prejudice reduction, and intergroup contact as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.C: Using only participants who responded most strongly would make the result more representative because it would maximize the size of the observed effect.
choices.D: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring hostile attribution score after reading ambiguous teammate feedback over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.

questions.passage: Feedback-based correction of implicit-bias errors. Researchers examined whether immediate feedback changes biased error patterns during rapid person-perception judgments using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 310 adults completing a speeded categorization task with race-neutral occupational cues and compared No feedback, Accuracy feedback, Feedback + reflection. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in whether participants received accuracy feedback and a structured reflection prompt after biased errors, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was percentage of bias-consistent errors during the second half of the task; a secondary measure was response latency and explicit egalitarian-attitude score. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring baseline implicit-association score and prior diversity training, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that bias-consistent errors decreased most when accuracy feedback was paired with reflection. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, practice effects or motivation to appear unbiased could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition reported greater change in bias-consistent errors and response latency than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether baseline implicit-association score altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be using a transfer task with new stimuli to test whether learning generalizes beyond the practiced categorization. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome.

choices.B: Showing that every participant preferred the intervention would prove the mechanism, even if preference was unrelated to percentage of bias-consistent errors during the second half of the task.
choices.C: Showing that baseline scores differed strongly between groups would strengthen the causal interpretation by making the groups easier to separate.
choices.D: Removing the manipulation check would strengthen the study because fewer measures always reduce the risk of measurement bias. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

explanations.B: This choice is tempting because it highlights a real issue in research on implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition, but it does not fit the passage as well as the credited answer. The option says, 'Showing that every participant preferred the intervention would prove the mechanism, even if preference was unrelated to percentage of bias-consistent errors during the second half of the task.' Yet the study manipulated whether participants received accuracy feedback and a structured reflection prompt after biased errors and measured percentage of bias-consistent errors during the second half of the task, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. The passage leaves room for practice effects or motivation to appear unbiased, but that limitation does not reverse the observed pattern or erase the construct being tested.

explanations.A: Correct. This answer best integrates the design, Figure 1, and the construct being tested. The passage says the groups differed in whether participants received accuracy feedback and a structured reflection prompt after biased errors and that the primary outcome was percentage of bias-consistent errors during the second half of the task; the figure then shows that bias-consistent errors decreased most when accuracy feedback was paired with reflection. That combination supports the inference in the answer without overstating it as proof of every possible mechanism. It also respects the distinction between a construct, such as implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition, and the way that construct was operationalized through bias-consistent errors and response latency and the measured outcome. questions.stem: Which limitation should most temper the authors' conclusion about implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition?

choices.A: The study is invalid because any use of a comparison condition prevents researchers from drawing conclusions about group differences. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.B: The design does not fully rule out practice effects or motivation to appear unbiased, so the result supports the authors' interpretation but does not uniquely identify one mechanism.
choices.C: The finding is impossible to interpret because a significant behavioral outcome can never coexist with an imperfect manipulation check.
choices.D: The result must be due to random error because all social and psychological interventions produce identical effects across participants.

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.B: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring percentage of bias-consistent errors during the second half of the task over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.
choices.C: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats implicit bias, controlled processing, and social cognition as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.D: Using only participants who responded most strongly would make the result more representative because it would maximize the size of the observed effect.
